speaker: interviewee
text: There I was to play one of the most difficult passages. I always sightread extremely well. As a matter of fact, it prevented me from practicing very hard. I learned that only later because I could manage. And also here, I immediately had seen this was a sort of sequence. And if you have a fingering for one group, it should fit for all. And I had the fingering for the first group, and I sailed through that passage absolutely as is if I would have never played anything else than this one. Now, I'm not exaggerating because there was a movie made of the Kulturbund that I told the whole story. And [? Schwartz, ?] the conductor saw that movie and said, that's absolutely right. I was amazed what he did. I thought he would fall flat on his face. That's why I ask him. But he played it brilliantly. So I'm very proud of that, and I was immediately accepted by all members of the orchestra, and I really had a very good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, how old were you, Mr. Meyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1923. 19-- 15, No? No a little bit more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, these are the ages where you do things like that. Later on when consciousness sets in, it becomes a little bit more difficult. You get a little bit more scared, but then [? not. ?] Well, anyway, the time in Berlin was excellent. Except the political situation got worse and worse. Now, Berlin of course, had a Jewish congregation then of about 180,000 people. And I was not from Berlin. And I always thought I wouldn't be noticed. And I did all kinds of crazy things. I went to rehearsals of the Berlin Philharmonia with Herbert von Karajan conducting. And there were bomb attacks, and we had to go into public bomb shelters. I could have found-- been found out. There was already curfew. We were not supposed to be out in the evening. Well, anyway the life was very nice. The director of the Jewish Kulturbund who also knew me was very fond of me, and he had a daughter I was very fond of. And so was she, and we are still today-- she survived-- very, very close friends. As a matter of fact, the end of the year's coming up, and I will be in Berlin as their guests.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Jewish Kulturbund Orchestra-- the Jewish Kulturbund wouldn't last much longer. This [NON-ENGLISH] star got into-- onto our clothing, and the Kulturbund was liquidated. But then the question was, should we return to Dresden to help our parents? Very soon the deportation started. And we thought that perhaps if all-- we both brothers would be with my parents, and we would be sent to a ghetto or something, situation would be easier for them. Well, my brother returned. I stayed on in Berlin, and I was put into the German war efforts. And I went to the-- Kulturbund finished, and I went to the organization which assigned you to jobs and said, please, could you give me some kind of work where I preserve my hands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Said, oh, sure, sure, sure. You're going to go to an factory which makes sanitary articles. I lived in the middle of Berlin, in the western part of Berlin, and this victory was all the way in the east. So one hour by subway, or S-Bahn. That's the one which went outside. And-- well, was in Kopenick. And Kopenick was known about two things. One, which was a famous play and movie and everything in Germany. And the other one was a factory called Fromm's Act.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Fromm's was the family who owned it. What the Act stands for, I really don't know. But it was the biggest producer of condoms. Now, at the age of 16, this was a very, very strange occupation. And especially if people ask, you know, what do you do? Well, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very embarrassed to say something about it. You must know that these days, these articles were not on sale anymore. They were reserved for the army because the normal population was sort of encouraged to produce children for the war effort also that something would be left. It was an unbelievable experience. And it became from strange to hilarious, and I don't think I should tell stories which happened there. Anyway, it was-- I only have to tell one story because it's so funny. I worked in the vulcanization of that. It's hard, very, very hard work. You have to wear gas masks, and you got special milk rationing, which we didn't get. And it was really, very, very, hard. And to have these things vulcanized, you had to put them into a big cart, on glass forms, but it was unvulcanized rubber then. Well, anyway, there were two models we did. One, the normal one. The other one, which had a sort of drop container at the tip. And I-- nobody told me what this was that. That-- so I put it all in. I put this cart into the oven. I closed the oven, and the oven had a fan which would go around so that the air would be-- go to all places. Well, I had put the ones with the drop container on top. And of course, into the reach of the fan. And within about one second I had ruined 2,000 of these items. Needless to say, I was not kept in that position. I was transferred to something else, but became sort of a very funny famous figure [? in the house. ?] Well, that-- it was very, very hard work. And pretty soon, I thought that's no use. I better return to Dresden. And I returned to Dresden and was immediately imprisoned. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My girlfriend, the daughter of the director of the Kulturbund, was partly Gentile. Her mother was Gentile, and she had the privilege of all Gentiles. And one of the-- the cook went to the Gestapo and said, look what she's doing. She has all the privileges, but the only think he can think of is a Jewish boyfriend. And we both were put into prison. She in Berlin and I in Dresden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], the big factory for optical instruments, employed all the Jews in Dresden, and they had heard about me as a young person. And I was immediately claimed by them, and I got out of camp-- I got out of prison. And worked at [INAUDIBLE]. Neither my father nor my mother worked there. They had other duties, and there came the first big wave of deportation as part of the final solution. And my parents were deported, and my brother and myself were left behind. So our plan to be with our parents was moot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they deported to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Riga. She transferred to Riga. My mother died in Riga of a heart attack. My father was transferred from Riga all over Germany to Dachau and died about two months before the liberation in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about their deaths?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are records in-- in Germany. And you have to-- if you make claims, you have to find out what this. But I knew about my parents in Riga because one, my mother was a cleaning woman for the officer's barrack and was very much liked. And one of the officers said he's going to Dresden, and so my mother said, well, wouldn't you like to see my sons and perhaps bring us some winter clothes or something like that? And he did. He appeared one evening, and we made suitcases and he went back to-- he had letters from my parents. This had-- these cases have happened in Germany all along. Doesn't excuse what the others did, but it shows a certain balance that there were decent citizens once in awhile. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I then heard that my mother had died of a heart attack. I don't know the date, but I heard that. I also heard that after the liberation. Some people survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is year before we were deported from Dresden-- was terrible in Dresden-- the Gestapo started visiting us practically every evening. And we were shifted from one Judenhaus to another. And our belongings which we had became less and less. But in the one first of the Judenhauses, we lived where my parents even had lived with us, there was a beautiful piano. I think it was my-- our piano in-- in-- [INAUDIBLE]. And my brother played, and I played. So when we had a minute and weren't too tired, we were doing it. And one evening, one of these bunch of SS characters from the Gestapo appeared, and my brother played. And it infuriated them so much that they took the piano, rolled it out on the porch, and then threw it overboard. Now, if you ever have heard the clatter and the terrible sound of a breaking piano, breaking up or something, you never forget it. I still have-- what should I say-- strange feelings when a piano is shifted on stage and one of the wheels comes close to the edge of the stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. Everywhere beating and all kinds of things. And then two nights later, another SS guy appears, an officer, who always came and his-- my brother played for him. He said, where's your piano? Now, you see, in normal times, you say, they-- they have done that. And it's out. But you're going to say that the SS has rolled it out on this-- on the porch and [INAUDIBLE]? I think it was still lying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, next day they brought a new Steinway grand because he wanted to hear my brother. And nobody ever touched that piano again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the situation. Another situation just to tell you what one went through, the former maid of us had brought us some sugar, and we knew very-- very well sugar was not for everybody. So we put it into a bowl which said 'salt' on top. Then a Gestapo came and beat us up and all kinds of things. And force my brother to eat this bowl of salt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Luckily enough, it was sugar, which was difficult enough. But imagine with salt, what that would do to your insides. They found a pair of boots. They looked new, but I had them for years. And they wouldn't believe me. They thought I bought them black market or somewhere, and they said you come next morning to visit us at the Gestapo headquarter. And I did, and they beat the hell out of me. And the one thing, which was left, is a slight hearing defect in my left ear which has not bothered me through my career because the-- it's in a frequency which was not the most difficult one. I'm-- I have trouble with high voices. Now, I'm an old man, and hearing is the first thing which disappears-- or one of the first things. So I can't really claim anymore, but it is the hearing gets worse. But so far, I still manage. I have to bother other people to repeat their sentences. Then-- this was life in Dresden. We were shifted from one house to the other. And then one day, it was all over, and we went to the Judensiedlung, S-I-E-D-L-U-N-G, in Hellerau, H-E-- double-L-- E-R-A-U. That was very close to the place where the factory was where we worked, and then we watched every morning there. We marched back, and we lived in these barracks. And by then, my brother had changed his piano into an accordion and started-- I had still the violin-- and we made music there. He transcribed everything for accordion with a keyboard. And we managed. But this wasn't for very long. We worked at [INAUDIBLE], as I said, and I actually a very agreeable work, nothing very difficult. We made parts of a clock for a time bomb. Now I know that. When I did this little thing there, I didn't know what it was for. But pretty soon, people told, and you found out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] More and more, we were replaced by Russian girls who came from somewhere. And it was absolutely fantastic to see. These girls were put the first time on the machine-- an big machines, complicated machines-- and somebody said, you do that. They'd say one-- one switch it off and switch it on. They immediately found something else and ruined the machine for good. These were very precise things to do. Within a day, nothing worked anymore in this factory. They must have been trained by somebody [? else who was ?] brilliant. Well, in our case, it was the first sign of we were sent somewhere else. And there came the day when we were deported to the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing we knew. We also didn't have an imagination big enough to-- to think about it. We were loaded for the first time in freight cars, you know, cattle cars. And that in itself was already a terrible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from the cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you. At first, I don't know how long we were in it. It got worse from minute to minute. It was cold. Sanitary conditions unbelievable without a-- half a day, everything was filled up and-- and flopping all over the car. It was-- I know that people died, had a heart attack or-- we had some elderly people in there too. My grandmother had been deported to Theresienstadt, and she died there. I know that because one can get records, and I even have her-- her death certificate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, after one day, two day-- no. It can't be. After two or three days, the morning was very cold. The train stopped, and I could see through a window which was in there, another locomotive standing on the other one and letting out steam. And through the steam, I saw a sign, Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know what this was. But the minute later, I found out. Doors were opened. We were told to leave everything lying there. It would be given to us at a later moment. Remember-- you know getting out [INAUDIBLE] [? lying around ?] there. And we were chased out of these cars, beaten, with dogs barking, and we were lined up. In front of an officer, who went this way and this way. And once in awhile, ask a question. Then they went that way. Anyway, I turned up on that side let's say-- don't remember-- with my brother. We were holding onto each other. And we were loaded on tracks, and we were carted off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we came to another camp where we were unloaded and ushered into barracks, us to undress, takes shower. We were-- all our hairs were cut from your head, all over the body. And we were given uniforms, you know, prison uniforms, striped ones. You know, this is yours. This is yours. This is yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Size! Nobody looking at that one. You know, some people couldn't get into anything they got, and others were slopping around in it. And it was terrible. And then we had to line up, and there were people who took notices what we-- our name, where came from, and so on and so on. And then came the famous moment where we [? ended up, ?] standing there, and they start to tattoo a number on our arm. Now, should I show you mine? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am-- it's a handmade number, before the stamps were introduced. And it's sort of an amateur. It didn't do too good a job. My brother had a three. He was standing on this side, you know, Fritz and Henry. And 50 years later, last year, through a publication, a book which he wrote himself, Ernest Michel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He mentions in the book that his number is 104995. He was standing right next to me. I didn't have the slightest idea about it. I called him in New York. He is the professional fundraiser for the United Jewish Appeal, very known man, a lovely guy. And we got together and just two days ago, I saw him the last time in New York. We wasn't very happy about it because sometimes if you meet somebody after so many years you say, oh, I've got-- now I've met him. Goodbye. That's not the case between us. And I'm sure he will be asked to testify too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There we are in Buna. It was a new camp, and it belonged to the jurisdiction of Auschwitz. And it was actually a factory or a factory was built for IG Farben. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, at first, my brother got sick. Few days after we got there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dysentery. That's something which killed people in no time whatsoever. He was in sad shape, and I left the morning the camp. And when I came back in the evening he wasn't there anymore. Later on, I found out what happens to people like that because I got in the same boat. And much, much later, I register-- I looked at the books. Germans have one law [SPEAKING GERMAN], order has to be. So it was all written down when he died. The only thing which was a lie, how he died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I was in camp, and I was in a working group which built a-- a-- a system which would lug all kinds of parts through the entire factory. Was about, well, 30, 40 feet above the ground. And I was hanging up there holding a counterweight to a [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was a Dutch citizen, and he was professional [INAUDIBLE]. I was not. And my counter weight wasn't very much at that moment, and it was cold. And I had no gloves, and I had nothing. And I was scared sitting up there. And I only said, if I fall down, it's not my fault. I didn't want to sit there in the first place. One comes with all kinds of silly ideas. Well, it didn't last very long, and I got as sick as my brother. And I landed up in the Krankenbau, you know, in the hospital section. And they one thing we feared all our life in camp is selection. The minute you were not of any value as far as work is concerned to the Germans, they did away with you. They had planned it. So I was sick. I was in a selection, and I knew was what happened to me. Next morning, I would be brought to Auschwitz. And further on to Birkenau and gassed. Well, in the Krankenbau in Auschwitz, a prison doctor, also a Jew, came around and saw that I was awake at night and started conversation with me. He said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cheer up. It will be better. I said, no. Won't be better. This is the end. I know what gives here. That is an-- a kind of camp which does away with people. And tomorrow I will be gassed, and that will be it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So obviously, that wasn't the right-- what should I say-- theme for conversation. And he switched around. He said, what have you been doing--? I said, I haven't been doing very much. I'm very young, and I was sort of talented musician. And I played concerts and things like that. And I said, what have you been doing? He said, well, I have just started to be a doctor. I said, where? He said, in Breslau. I said, Breslau, I used to play. I played a concert in Breslau. And-- and the guy stopped and said, are you the one who played the Tartini Concerto in D minor? I said, yes. That's me. Well, I was at that concert. And he said, well, wait a minute. Wait a minute. Where could I have gone? I waited a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man disappeared from the barrack about-- I don't know when-- came back and had a body, a skeleton, a body, over his shoulder. Plunked this dead person next to me, took me over the shoulder, and carried me out of the Krankenbau into another barrack where he has brought this dead person from. He exchanged the registration cards, and I was alive again. He helped me with medication, and somehow I retrieved my strength. He got me an excellent job in Auschwitz. I-- I-- I became the [GERMAN], or whatever you call-- the janitor of the barracks for the SS guards. It was a barrack for the SS doctors. And one of my famous customer was Mengele. I made his bed, and I shined his shoes. And I had a hell of a good time. A hell of a good time. This time I really mean hell. But it was better because I had an-- a-- a jacket, warm. I didn't have to be outside. It was a wonderful job, and I thought I would make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop right here. We'll take a break here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
